INTRODUCTION             Introduction                                                      The game assumes the use of miniatures and a battle grid, and
                                                                                        the rules are written from this perspective.
              This is the DUNGEONS & DRAGONS® Roleplaying Game, the game
              that defines the genre and has set the standard for fantasy role-                            CHARACTERS
              playing for more than 30 years.
                                                                                        Your characters star in the adventures you play, just like the heroes
                 D&D® is a game of your imagination in which you participate            of a book or movie. As a player, you create a character using the
              in thrilling adventures and dangerous quests by taking on the role        rules in this book. Your character might be a savage barbarian
              of a hero--a character you create. Your character might be a strong       from the frozen wastes or a clever rogue with a quick wit and a
              fighter or a clever rogue, a devout cleric or a powerful wizard.          quicker blade. You might be a deadly archer trained in survival
              With a few trusted allies at your side, you explore ruins and mon-        techniques or a wizard who has mastered the arcane arts. As your
              ster-filled dungeons in search of treasure. The game offers endless       character participates in adventures, he or she gains experience
              possibilities and a multitude of choices--more choices than even          and becomes more powerful.
              the most sophisticated computer game, because you can do what-
              ever you can imagine.                                                                        ADVENTURES

                              THE D&D GAME                                              Your character is an adventurer, a hero who sets out on epic quests
                                                                                        for fortune and glory. Other characters join your adventuring
              The D&D game is a fantasy game of your imagination. It's part acting,     party to explore dungeons and battle monsters such as the terrible
              part storytelling, part social interaction, part war game, and part dice  dragon or the carnivorous troll. These quests unfold as stories cre-
              rolling. You and your friends create characters that develop and grow     ated by the actions your characters perform and the situations
              with each adventure they complete. One player is the Dungeon              your DM presents.
              Master (DM). The DM controls the monsters and enemies, narrates
              the action, referees the game, and sets up the adventures. Together,         A DUNGEONS & DRAGONS adventure features plenty of action,
              the Dungeon Master and the players make the game come alive.              exciting combat, terrifying monsters, epic challenges, and all
                                                                                        kinds of mysteries to uncover. What lies at the heart of the dun-
                 This Player's Handbook has all the rules players need to create        geon? What waits around the next corner or behind the next
              characters, select equipment, and engage in combat with a variety         door? Playing the roles of your characters, you and your friends
              of supernatural and mythical foes.                                        face the dangers and explore a world of medieval fantasy.

                 The Dungeon Master's Guide, available separately, provides the DM         One adventure might play out in a single game session; another
              with advice, guidelines, and everything he or she needs to create         might stretch across several sessions of play. A session lasts as long
              challenges, adventures, and full-fledged D&D campaigns, includ-           as you and your friends want to play, from a couple of hours to an
              ing sections on prestige classes, magic items, and character rewards.     all-day affair. The game can be stopped at any time and picked up
                                                                                        wherever you left off when everyone gets back together.
                 The Monster Manual, available separately, contains material that
              players and DMs alike will find useful. With hundreds of mon-                Every adventure is different, every quest unique. Your character
              sters to populate all levels of dungeons, this tome also includes         might explore ancient ruins guarded by devious traps or loot the
              monster creation rules, information on playing monsters as char-          tomb of a long-forgotten wizard. You might sneak into a castle to
              acters, details on monster tactics, and powered-up versions of stan-      spy on an enemy or face the life-draining touch of an undead crea-
              dard creatures.                                                           ture. Anything is possible in a DUNGEONS & DRAGONS game, and
                                                                                        your character can try to do anything you can imagine.
                 Together, these three volumes comprise the core rules for the
              DUNGEONS & DRAGONS game.

                       THREE DIMENSIONS                                                          PLAYING THE GAME

tens digit in tens (00, 10, 20, etc.) and the ones digit in ones (0, 1, 2, THE PLAYER'S ROLE

etc.). In this case, a roll of 70 and 1 is 71, and 00 and 0 is 100.    As a player, you use this handbook to create and run a character.

              STRENGTH (STR)                                                               Dexterity measures hand-eye coordination, agility, reflexes, and bal-
                                                                                           ance. This ability is the most important one for rogues, but it's also

              Strength measures your character's muscle and physical power. high on the list for characters who typically wear light or medium

              This ability is especially important for fighters, barbarians, pal- armor (rangers and barbarians) or no armor at all (monks, wizards,

              adins, rangers, and monks because it helps them prevail in and sorcerers), and for anyone who wants to be a skilled archer.

              combat. Strength also limits the amount of equipment your char-              You apply your character's Dexterity modifier to:

              INTELLIGENCE, WISDOM, AND CHARISMA                                          Charisma knows enough to speak carefully and may become an advi-

              You can use your character's Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma sor (or "power behind the throne") rather than a leader. The wise char-

              scores to guide you in roleplaying your character. Here is some back- acter lacking in both Intelligence and Charisma is uncouth and unso-

              ground (just guidelines) about what these scores can mean.                  phisticated.

              A smart character (one with high Intelligence) is curious, knowledge- A character with a low Wisdom score may be rash, imprudent, irre-

              able, and prone to using big words. A character with a high Intelligence but sponsible, or "out of it."

              low Wisdom may be smart but absentminded, or knowledgeable but lack- A character with high Charisma may be attractive, striking, person-

              ing in common sense. A character with high Intelligence but low Charisma able, and confident. A character with high Charisma but low

              may be a know-it-all or a reclusive scholar. A smart character lacking in Intelligence can usually pass herself off as knowledgeable, until she

              both Wisdom and Charisma may put her foot in her mouth often.               meets a true expert. A charismatic character with low Wisdom may be

              A character with low Intelligence mispronounces and misuses popular, but she doesn't know who her real friends are. A charismatic

              words, has trouble following directions, or fails to get the joke.          character lacking in both Intelligence and Wisdom is likely to be shal-

              A character with high Wisdom may be sensible, serene, "in tune," low and unaware of others' feelings.

              alert, or centered. A character with high Wisdom but low Intelligence A character with low Charisma may be reserved, gruff, rude, fawning,

              may be aware, but simple. A character with high Wisdom but low or simply nondescript.

14                                                                                                               DWARF RACIAL TRAITS

                                                                                                                    · +2 Constitution, ­2 Charisma: Dwarves are stout and tough but
                                                                                                                       tend to be gruff and reserved.

                                                                                                                    · Medium: As Medium creatures, dwarves have no special
                                                                                                                       bonuses or penalties due to their size.

                                                                                                                    · Dwarf base land speed is 20 feet. However, dwarves can move at
                                                                                                                       this speed even when wearing medium or heavy armor or when
                                                                                                                       carrying a medium or heavy load (unlike other creatures, whose
                                                                                                                       speed is reduced in such situations).

                                                                                                                    · Darkvision: Dwarves can see in the dark up to 60 feet. Dark-
                                                                                                                       vision is black and white only, but it is otherwise like normal
                                                                                                                       sight, and dwarves can function just fine with no light at all.

               Clerics sometimes receive orders, or at least suggestions, from
            their ecclesiastical superiors, directing them to undertake mis-
            sions for the church. The clerics and their companions are com-
            pensated fairly for these missions, and the church may be espe-
            cially generous with the casting of needed spells or divine magic
            items as payment.

                                  can cast, how many spells she can cast per day, and how hard those        Spells: A druid casts divine spells (the same type of spells avail-
                                  spells are to resist. To cast a spell, a druid must have a Wisdom       able to the cleric, paladin, and ranger), which are drawn from the
                                  score of 10 + the spell's level. A druid gets bonus spells based on     druid spell list (page 189). Her alignment may restrict her from
                                                                                                          casting certain spells opposed to her moral or ethical beliefs; see
                                                                                                          Chaotic, Evil, Good, and Lawful Spells, below. A druid must
                                                                                                          choose and prepare her spells in advance (see below).

                                                                                                          equal to at least 10 + the spell level (Wis 10 for 0-level spells, Wis
                                                                                                          11 for 1st-level spells, and so forth). The Difficulty Class for a
                                                                                                          saving throw against a druid's spell is 10 + the spell level + the
                                                                                                          druid's Wisdom modifier.
                                                                                                             Like other spellcasters, a druid can cast only a certain number
                                                                                                          of spells of each spell level per day. Her base daily spell allotment
                                                                                                          is given on Table 3­8: The Druid. In addition, she receives bonus
                                                                                                          spells per day if she has a high Wisdom score (see Table 1­1: Abil-
                                                                                                          ity Modifiers and Bonus Spells, page 8). She does not have access
                                                                                                          to any domain spells or granted powers, as a cleric does.

                                                                                  Characteristics: Of all the classes, the fighter has the best all-
                                                                               around fighting capabilities (hence the name). Fighters are
                                                                               familiar with all the standard weapons and armors. In addition to
                                   general fighting prowess, each fighter develops particular spe-          ranged combat can prove very deadly, though without other melee
                                   cialties of his own. A given fighter may be especially capable           support, they can find themselves in front-line combat more often
                                   with certain weapons; another might be trained to execute spe-           than they might prefer.
                                   cific fancy maneuvers. As fighters gain experience, they get
                                   more opportunities to develop their fighting skills. Thanks to           GAME RULE INFORMATION
                                   their focus on combat maneuvers, they can master the most dif-
                                   ficult ones relatively quickly.                                          Fighters have the following game statistics.

Base                    Fort   Ref Will                                    Dotted across the landscape are monasteries--small, walled clois-
                                                                           ters inhabited by monks who pursue personal perfection through
Level Attack Bonus      Save   Save Save Special                           action as well as contemplation. They train themselves to be versa-
                                                                           tile warriors skilled at fighting without weapons or armor. The
1st +1                  +2     +0       +0 Bonus feat                      inhabitants of monasteries headed by good masters serve as pro-
                                                                           tectors of the people. Ready for battle even when barefoot and
2nd +2                  +3     +0       +0 Bonus feat                      dressed in peasant clothes, monks can travel unnoticed among the
                                                                           populace, catching bandits, warlords, and corrupt nobles
3rd +3                  +3     +1       +1                                 unawares. In contrast, the residents of monasteries headed by evil
                                                                           masters rule the surrounding lands through fear, as an evil war-
4th +4                  +4     +1       +1 Bonus feat                      lord and his entourage might. Evil monks make ideal spies, infil-
                                                                           trators, and assassins.

                                                                              An individual monk is unlikely to care passionately about
6th +6/+1               +5     +2       +2 Bonus feat                      championing commoners or amassing wealth. She cares primarily

7th +7/+2               +5     +2       +2                                 Her goal is to achieve a state that is beyond the mortal realm.

                                                                           personal test. While not prone to showing off, monks are willing

                                                                           are not greedy for material wealth, but they eagerly seek that

11th +11/+6/+1          +7     +3       +3                                    Characteristics: The key feature of the monk is her ability to
                                                                           fight unarmed and unarmored. Thanks to her rigorous training,
12th +12/+7/+2          +8     +4       +4 Bonus feat                      she can strike as hard as if she were armed and strike faster than a
                                                                           warrior with a sword.
13th +13/+8/+3          +8     +4       +4
                                                                              Though a monk casts no spells, she channels a subtle energy,
14th +14/+9/+4          +9     +4       +4 Bonus feat                      called ki, which allows her to perform amazing feats. The monk's
                                                                           best-known feat is her ability to stun an opponent with an
15th +15/+10/+5         +9     +5       +5                                 unarmed blow. A monk also has a preternatural awareness that
                                                                           allows her to dodge an attack even if she is not consciously aware
16th +16/+11/+6/+1      +10    +5       +5 Bonus feat                      of it.

17th +17/+12/+7/+2      +10    +5       +5                                    As a monk gains experience and power, her mundane and ki-
                                                                           oriented abilities grow, giving her more and more power over her-
18th +18/+13/+8/+3      +11    +6       +6 Bonus feat                      self and, sometimes, over others.

bonus.

Unarmed Strike: Monks are highly

trained in fighting unarmed, giving them

considerable advantages when doing so. At

1st level, a monk gains Improved

Unarmed Strike as a bonus feat. A

monk's attacks may be with either fist

interchangeably or even from elbows, knees,

and feet. This means that a monk may even make

unarmed strikes with her hands full. There is no

such thing as an off-hand attack for a monk strik-

ing unarmed. A monk may thus apply her full

Strength bonus on damage rolls for all her unarmed strikes.

Usually a monk's unarmed strikes deal lethal damage, but she

can choose to deal nonlethal damage instead with no penalty on

her attack roll. She has the same choice to deal lethal or nonlethal

damage while grappling (see page 156).

A monk's unarmed strike is treated both as a manufactured

weapon and a natural weapon for the purpose of spells and effects

that enhance or improve either manufactured weapons or natural

weapons (such as the magic fang and magic weapon spells).

A monk also deals more damage with her unarmed strikes than

a normal person would, as shown on Table 3­10: The Monk. The

unarmed damage on Table 3­10 is for Medium monks. A Small

monk deals less damage than the amount given there with her

unarmed attacks, while a Large monk deals more damage; see

Table 3­11: Small or Large Monk Unarmed Damage.                                                                                                 41

               equal to at least 10 + the spell level (Wis 11 for 1st-level spells, Wis          Associates: While she may adventure with characters of any

                                                                                           Ex-Paladins
                                                                                              A paladin who ceases to be lawful good, who willfully commits an
                                                                                              evil act, or who grossly violates the code of conduct loses all pal-
                                                                                              adin spells and abilities (including the service of the paladin's

                                                                                Alignment: Rangers can be of any alignment. Most are good,

                                                                                and such rangers usually function as protectors of the wild areas. In

                                                                                              this role, a ranger seeks out and destroys

                                                                                    Soveliss  or drives off evil creatures that threaten

                                                                                              the wilderness. Good rangers also pro-

                                                                                Track: A ranger gains Track (see page 101) as a bonus feat.          47
                                                                                Wild Empathy (Ex): A ranger can use body language, vocaliza-
                                                                             tions, and demeanor to improve the attitude of an animal (such as
                                                                             a bear or a monitor lizard). This ability functions just like a Diplo-
                                                                             macy check to improve the attitude of a person (see page 72). The
                                                                             ranger rolls 1d20 and adds his ranger level and his Charisma mod-
                                                                             ifier to determine the wild empathy check result. The typical
                                                                             domestic animal has a starting attitude of indifferent, while wild
                                                                             animals are usually unfriendly.
                                                                                To use wild empathy, the ranger and the animal must be able to
                                                                             study each other, which means that they must be within 30 feet
                                                                             of one another under normal visibility conditions. Generally,
CHAPTER 3:  influencing an animal in this way takes 1 minute, but, as with               list, provided that he can cast spells of that level, but he must

Rogues have little in common with one another. Some are stealthy           Rogues do not see each other as fellows unless they happen to
thieves. Others are silver-tongued tricksters. Still others are scouts,  be members of the same guild or students of the same mentor. In
infiltrators, spies, diplomats, or thugs. What they do share is versa-   fact, rogues trust other rogues less than they trust anyone else.
tility, adaptability, and resourcefulness. In general, rogues are        They're no fools.
skilled at getting what others don't want them to get: entrance into
a locked treasure vault, safe passage past a deadly trap, secret battle    Races: Adaptable and often unprincipled, humans take to the
plans, a guard's trust, or some random person's pocket money.            rogue's life with ease. Halflings, elves, and half-elves also find
                                                                         themselves well suited to the demands of this career. Dwarf and
   Adventures: Rogues adventure for the same reason they do              gnome rogues, while less common, are renowned as experts with
most other things: to get what they can get. Some are after loot;        locks and traps. Half-orc rogues tend toward thuggery.
others want experience. Some crave fame; others seek infamy.

trap or avoid an alarm is great fun for many rogues.                     lins and bugbears. Rogues who learn their arts in savage lands,

   Characteristics: Rogues are highly skilled, and they can con-         mechanisms such as traps and locks.
centrate on developing any of several categories of skills. While
not equal to members of many other classes in combat, a rogue              Other Classes: Rogues love and hate working with members

damage with a sneak attack.                                              ported by spellcasters. There are plenty of times, however, that

master the arts of stealth, evasion, and sneak attacks. In addition,     unavoidable.
while not capable of casting spells on their own, rogues can "fake

just about any other magic item.                                         her skill selection--from charismatic con artist to cunning burglar
                                                                         to agile combatant--but most rogues share certain aspects. They

                                                                         tunistic sneak attacks or ranged attacks. The rogue's stealth and her
                                                                         trapfinding ability make her one of the best scouts in the game.

                                 Should the rogue score a critical hit with a sneak attack, this extra

                                 damage is not multiplied. (See Table 8­5: Attack Roll Modifiers

                                 and Table 8­6: Armor Class Modifiers, page 151, for combat situa-

                                 tions in which the rogue flanks an opponent or the opponent loses

                                 his Dexterity bonus to AC.)

                                 Ranged attacks can count as sneak attacks only if the target is

                                 within 30 feet. A rogue can't strike with deadly accuracy from

                                 beyond that range.

                                 With a sap (blackjack) or an unarmed strike, a rogue can make a

                                 sneak attack that deals nonlethal damage instead of lethal damage.

                                 She cannot use a weapon that deals lethal damage to deal non-

                                 lethal damage in a sneak attack, not even with the usual ­4

                                 penalty, because she must make optimal use of her weapon in

                                 order to execute a sneak attack. (See Nonlethal Damage, page 146.)

                                 A rogue can sneak attack only living creatures with discernible

                                 anatomies--undead, constructs, oozes, plants, and incorporeal

                                 creatures lack vital areas to attack. Any creature that is immune to

                                                                                           Adventures: The typical sorcerer

                                                                                           adventures in order to improve his

                                                                                           abilities. Only by testing his limits

                                                                                           can he surpass them. A sorcerer's power

                                                                                           is inborn--part of his soul. Developing this

                                                                                           power is a quest in itself for many sorcer-

                                                                                           ers, regardless of how they wish to use

                                                                                           their power.

                                                                                           Some good sorcerers are driven by

                                                                         the need to prove themselves. Marked as different by their power,

                                                                         they seek to win places in society and to prove themselves to

                                                                         others. Evil sorcerers also feel themselves set apart from others--

                                                                         apart and above. They adventure to gain power over those they

                                                                         look down upon.                                                      51

            4th   6 3 1 --------------                                                      1st-level spells per day--three for being 1st level (see Table 3­16:

            6th   7 4 2 1 ------------                                                      Table 3­17: Sorcerer Spells Known). Thus, on any given day, he

            8th   8 5 3 2 1 ----------                                                         Familiar: A sorcerer can obtain a familiar. Doing so takes 24
                                                                                            hours and uses up magical materials that cost 100 gp. A familiar is
            9th   8 5 4 3 2 ----------                                                      a magical beast that resembles a small animal and is unusually
                                                                                            tough and intelligent. The creature serves as a companion and
            10th  9 5 4 3 2 1 --------                                                      servant.

            11th  9 5 5 4 3 2 --------                                                         The sorcerer chooses the kind of familiar he gets. As the sor-
                                                                                            cerer advances in level, his familiar also increases in power.
            12th  9 5 5 4 3 2 1 ------
                                                                                               If the familiar dies or is dismissed by the sorcerer, the sorcerer
            13th  9 5 5 4 4 3 2 ------                                                      must attempt a DC 15 Fortitude saving throw. Failure means he
                                                                                            loses 200 experience points per sorcerer level; success reduces the
            14th  9 5 5 4 4 3 2 1 ----                                                      loss to one-half that amount. However, a sorcerer's experience
                                                                                            point total can never go below 0 as the result of a familiar's demise
            15th  9 5 5 4 4 4 3 2 ----                                                      or dismissal. For example, suppose that Hennet is a 3rd-level sor-
                                                                                            cerer with 3,230 XP when his owl familiar is killed by a bugbear.
            16th  9 5 5 4 4 4 3 2 1--                                                       Hennet makes a successful saving throw, so he loses 300 XP, drop-
                                                                                            ping him below 3,000 XP and back to 2nd level (see the Dungeon
            17th  9 5 5 4 4 4 3 3 2--                                                       Master's Guide for rules for losing levels). A slain or dismissed
                                                                                            familiar cannot be replaced for a year and day. A slain familiar can
            18th  9554443321                                                                be raised from the dead just as a character can be, and it does not
                                                                                            lose a level or a Constitution point when this happy event occurs.

               available to bards and wizards), which are drawn primarily from                 Skill Selection: Pick a number of skills equal to 3 + Int modifier.
               the sorcerer/wizard spell list (page 192). He can cast any spell he
               knows without preparing it ahead of time, the way a wizard or a
               cleric must (see below).
                  To learn or cast a spell, a sorcerer must have a Charisma score
               equal to at least 10 + the spell level (Cha 10 for 0-level spells, Cha
               11 for 1st-level spells, and so forth). The Difficulty Class for a
               saving throw against a sorcerer's spell is 10 + the spell level + the
               sorcerer's Charisma modifier.
                  Like other spellcasters, a sorcerer can cast only a certain
               number of spells of each spell level per day. His base daily spell
               allotment is given on Table 3­16: The Sorcerer. In addition, he
               receives bonus spells per day if he has a high Charisma score (see
               Table 1­1: Ability Modifiers and Bonus Spells, page 8).
                  A sorcerer's selection of spells is extremely limited. A sorcerer
               begins play knowing four 0-level spells (also called cantrips) and
               two 1st-level spells of your choice. At each new sorcerer level, he
               gains one or more new spells, as indicated on Table 3­17: Sorcerer

than a battleaxe, when they are the words and gestures of a wizard.    Background: Wizards recognize each other as comrades or

            ARCANE SPELLS AND ARMOR                                                 By contrast, bards not only know how to wear light armor effectively,

            Wizards and sorcerers do not know how to wear armor effectively. but they can also ignore the arcane spell failure chance for such armor.

            If desired, they can wear armor anyway (though they'll be clumsy in However, they too wear heavier armor ineffectively and must either

            it), or they can gain training in the proper use of armor (with the learn to wear heavier armor via the appropriate Armor Proficiency feat

            the Shield Proficiency feat), or they can multiclass to add a class such proficiency as a class feature. A bard wearing armor heavier than

            that grants them armor proficiency (see Multiclass Characters later light or using any type of shield incurs the normal arcane spell failure

            in this chapter). Even if a wizard or sorcerer is wearing armor with chance, even if he becomes proficient with that armor.

Scribe Scroll: At 1st level, a wizard gains

Scribe Scroll as a bonus feat. This feat enables

her to create magic scrolls (see Scribe Scroll, page 99, and

Creating Magic Items, page 282 of the Dun-

geon Master's Guide).

level, a wizard gains a bonus feat. At each

such opportunity, she can choose a

metamagic feat, an item creation feat,

or Spell Mastery. The wizard must

still meet all prerequisites for a bonus

feat, including caster level mini-

mums. (See Chapter 5 for descrip-

tions of feats and their prerequisites.)

SCHOOL SPECIALIZATION                                           mutation. Spells that do not fall into any of these schools are called

A school is one of eight groupings of spells, each defined by a common universal spells.

theme, such as illusion or necromancy. If desired, a wizard may Abjuration: Spells that protect, block, or banish. An abjuration

specialize in one school of magic (see below). Specialization allows a specialist is called an abjurer.

wizard to cast extra spells from her chosen school, but she then never Conjuration: Spells that bring creatures or materials to the caster. A

learns to cast spells from some other schools. Essentially, the wizard conjuration specialist is called a conjurer.

gains exceptional mastery over a single school by neglecting the study Divination: Spells that reveal information. A divination specialist is

            Druid      (2 + Int modifier) × 4            2 + Int modifier                     out by 1 rank or any cross-class skill that you've previously maxed

            Paladin    (4 + Int modifier) × 4            4 + Int modifier                     points on it and increase its rank further.
            Ranger     (2 + Int modifier) × 4            2 + Int modifier                        First, find out what your character's maximum rank in that skill
            Rogue      (6 + Int modifier) × 4            6 + Int modifier                     is. If it's a class skill, the maximum rank is the character's new level
            Sorcerer   (8 + Int modifier) × 4            8 + Int modifier                     + 3. If it's a cross-class skill, the maximum rank is half of that
            Wizard     (2 + Int modifier) × 4            2 + Int modifier                     number (do not round up or down).
                       (2 + Int modifier) × 4            2 + Int modifier                        You may spend the number of skill points it takes to max out

            Trying Again
               In general, you can try a skill check again if you fail, and you can
               keep trying indefinitely. Some skills, however, have consequences
               of failure that must be taken into account. A few skills are virtually
               useless once a check has failed on an attempt to accomplish a par-
               ticular task. For most skills, when a character has succeeded once
               at a given task, additional successes are meaningless.
                  For example, if Lidda the rogue misses an Open Lock check, she
               can try again and keep trying. If, however, a trap in the lock goes
               off if she misses an Open Lock check by 5 or more, then failure has
               its own penalties.
                  Similarly, if Lidda misses a Climb check, she can keep trying,
               but if she misses by 5 or more, she falls (after which she can get up
               and try again).
                  If Tordek has negative hit points and is dying, Lidda can make
               an untrained Heal check to make him stable. If the check fails,
               Tordek probably loses another hit point, but Lidda can try again in
               the next round.

      Taking 10: When your character is not being threatened or dis-         COMBINING SKILL ATTEMPTS
   tracted, you may choose to take 10. Instead of rolling 1d20 for the
   skill check, calculate your result as if you had rolled a 10. For many       When more than one character tries the same skill at the same
   routine tasks, taking 10 makes them automatically successful. Dis-           time and for the same purpose, their efforts may overlap.
   tractions or threats (such as combat) make it impossible for a char-
   acter to take 10. In most cases, taking 10 is purely a safety meas-       Individual Events
   ure--you know (or expect) that an average roll will succeed but              Often, several characters attempt some action and each succeeds
   fear that a poor roll might fail, so you elect to settle for the average     or fails independently.
   roll (a 10). Taking 10 is especially useful in situations where a par-          For example, Krusk and each of his friends needs to climb a

               only a limited number of characters can help at once. The DM            ABILITY CHECKS

            Skill Synergy
               It's possible for a character to have two skills that work well         Task                                    Key Ability
               together, such as someone with both Jump and Tumble. In gen-            Breaking open a jammed or locked door1  Strength

      Untrained: For common items, failure on an untrained check             BLUFF (CHA)
   means no estimate. For rare items, success means an estimate of
   50% to 150% (2d6+3 times 10%).                                           You can make the outrageous or the untrue seem plausible, or
                                                                            use doublespeak or innuendo to deliver a secret message to
                                                                            another character. The skill encompasses acting, conning,
                                                                            fast talking, misdirection, prevarication, and misleading
                                                                            body language. Use a bluff to sow temporary confusion, get
                                                                            someone to turn and look where you point, or simply look
                                                                            innocuous.

                                                                            check for each one.

            PLAYER KNOWLEDGE VERSUS                                                   Generally speaking, it's impossible to separate completely your

            CHARACTER KNOWLEDGE                                                       personal knowledge (also called player knowledge) from your charac-

            It's pretty simple to measure a character's knowledge of things the ter's knowledge. Ultimately, the decision on how (or if) to divide

            player doesn't know. That's what a Knowledge skill check represents-- player knowledge from character knowledge must be made between

            for instance, the player of a character with many ranks in Knowledge the players and the DM. Some DMs encourage knowledgeable players

            (geography) isn't required to memorize all the geographical data about to use their experience to help their characters succeed. Others prefer

            the campaign world to use his character's skill ranks.                    that characters display only the knowledge represented by their skill

            The opposite case, however, is harder to adjudicate cleanly. What ranks and other game statistics. Most fall somewhere between those

            happens when a player knows something that his or her character does two extremes.

            perienced characters don't know that fact.                                this topic.

                                                                               Special: A bard must have at least 3 ranks in a Perform skill to
   You can pick padlocks, finesse combination locks, and solve              inspire courage in his allies, or to use his countersong or his fasci-

   tool is employed. If you use masterwork thieves' tools, you gain a
   +2 circumstance bonus on the check.

                                                                                    WHIRLWIND ATTACK [GENERAL]

                                                                                       You can strike nearby opponents in an amazing, spinning attack.
                                                                                          Prerequisites: Dex 13, Int 13, Combat Expertise, Dodge,

                                                                                       Mobility, Spring Attack, base attack bonus +4.
                                                                                          Benefit: When you use the full attack action, you can give up

                                                                                       your regular attacks and instead make one melee attack at your
                                                                                       full base attack bonus against each opponent within reach.

                                                                                          When you use the Whirlwind Attack feat, you also forfeit any
                                                                                       bonus or extra attacks granted by other feats or abilities (such as
                                                                                       the Cleave feat or the haste spell).

                                                                                          Special: A fighter may select Whirlwind Attack as one of his
                                                                                       fighter bonus feats (see page 38).

                                                                                    WIDEN SPELL [METAMAGIC]

                                                                                       You can increase the area of your spells.
                                                                                          Benefit: You can alter a burst, emanation, line, or spread-

                                                                                       shaped spell to increase its area. Any numeric measurements of
                                                                                       the spell's area increase by 100%. For example, a fireball spell
                                                                                       (which normally produces a 20-foot-radius spread) that is widened
                                                                                       now fills a 40-foot-radius spread. A widened spell uses up a spell
                                                                                       slot three levels higher than the spell's actual level.

                                                                                          Spells that do not have an area of one of these four sorts are not
                                                                                       affected by this feat.

                                                                     Choose an alignment for your character, using his or
   Devils in human guise stalk the land, tempting people
toward evil. Holy clerics use the power of good to protect wor-      her race and class as a guide. Most player characters are
shipers. Devotees of evil gods bring ruin on innocents to win
                                                                     good or neutral rather than evil. In general, evil align-

                                                                     ments are for villains and monsters.

                                                                     Alignment is a tool for developing your character's

                                                                     identity. It is not a straitjacket for restricting your

                                                                     character. Each alignment represents a broad range

                                                                     of personality types or personal philosophies, so two

                                                                     lawful good characters can still be quite different

                                                                     from each other. In addition, few people are com-

                                                                     pletely consistent. A lawful good character may

                                                                     have a greedy streak that occasionally tempts him to

                                                                     take something or hoard something he has, even if

                                                                     that's not lawful or good behavior. People are also not

                                                                     consistent from day to day. A good character can lose

                                                                     his temper, a neutral character can be inspired to           103
                                                                     perform a noble act, and so on.
CHAPTER 6:            Choosing an alignment for your character means stating your             homeland, but he would not do so for strangers who are not
                   intent to play that character a certain way. If your character acts in     related to him.
   DESCRIPTION     a way more appropriate to another alignment, the DM may decide
                   that his alignment has changed to match his actions.                          Being good or evil can be a conscious choice, as with the pal-
                                                                                              adin who attempts to live up to her ideals or the evil cleric who
                TYPICAL ALIGNMENTS                                                            causes pain and terror to emulate his god. For most people,
                                                                                              though, being good or evil is an attitude that one recognizes but
                   Creatures and members of classes shown in italic type on Table 6­1         does not choose. Being neutral on the good­evil axis usually rep-
                   are always of the indicated alignment. Except for paladins, they are       resents a lack of commitment one way or the other, but for some it
                   born into that alignment. It is inherent, part of their nature. Usu-       represents a positive commitment to a balanced view. While
                   ally, a creature with an inherent alignment has some connection            acknowledging that good and evil are objective states, not just
                   (through ancestry, history, or magic) to the Outer Planes or is a          opinions, these folk maintain that a balance between the two is the
                   magical beast.                                                             proper place for people, or at least for them.

                   Good characters and creatures protect innocent life. Evil charac-          Nine distinct alignments define all the possible combinations of
                   ters and creatures debase or destroy innocent life, whether for fun        the lawful­chaotic axis with the good­evil axis. Each alignment
                   or profit.                                                                 description below depicts a typical character of that alignment.
                                                                                              Remember that individuals vary from this norm, and that a given
                      "Good" implies altruism, respect for life, and a concern for the        character may act more or less in accord with his or her alignment
                   dignity of sentient beings. Good characters make personal sacri-           from day to day. Use these descriptions as guidelines, not as
                   fices to help others.                                                      scripts.

                   killing for sport or out of duty to some evil deity or master.

   Some neutral characters, on the other hand, commit them-                                       life. He plays by the rules but with-
selves philosophically to neutrality. They see good, evil, law, and

                                                                                                  fortable in a hierarchy and would

                                                                     lawful evil villains have particular taboos, such as not killing in

                                                                     cold blood (but having underlings do it) or not letting children

                                                                     come to harm (if it can be helped). They imagine that these com-

                                                                     punctions put them above unprincipled villains. The scheming

                                                                     baron who expands his power and exploits his people is lawful

                                                                     evil.

                                                                     Some lawful evil people and creatures commit themselves to

                                                                     evil with a zeal like that of a crusader committed to good. Beyond

                                                                     being willing to hurt others for their own ends, they take pleasure

                                                                     in spreading evil as an end unto itself. They may also see doing evil

                                                                     as part of a duty to an evil deity or master.

                                                                     Lawful evil is sometimes called "diabolical," because devils are

                                                                     the epitome of lawful evil.                                               105

                                                                                                                               Equipment: Your equipment can look the way you

                                                                                                                         want it to look to match your character's style. One

                                                                                                                wizard's quarterstaff might be a plain, straight length of wood,

                                                                                                                while another wizard's is gnarled, twisted, and engraved with

                                                                                                                mystic runes.

                                                                                                                Your character might have some items that aren't on the equip-

                                                                                                                ment lists (see Chapter 7). Agree with your DM on what a new

                                                                                                                item would do and how much it would cost, and then your char-

                                                                                                                acter can have it.

                                                                                                                Sometimes you see a weapon in a movie or read about one in a

                                                                                                                book, and you want your character to use that weapon. If it's not

                                                                                                                on the weapon list in Chapter 7, try to find a weapon on the list

                                                                                                                that seems equivalent. A katana (samurai sword), for example, is

                                                                                                                not on the weapon list, but you could equip your character with a

110                                                                                                             katana and just treat it like a masterwork bastard sword.

              COINS
                                                                                                  Trade goods are the exception to the half-price rule. A trade
                 The most common coin that adventurers use is the gold piece (gp).             good, in this sense, is a valuable good that can be easily exchanged
                 With 1 gold piece, a character can buy a belt pouch, 50 feet of               almost as if it were cash itself. Wheat, flour, cloth, gems, jewelry,
                 hempen rope, or a goat. A skilled (but not exceptional) artisan can           art objects, and valuable metals are trade goods, and merchants
                 earn 1 gold piece a day. The gold piece is the standard unit of meas-         often trade in them directly without using currency (see Table
                 ure for wealth. When merchants discuss deals that involve goods               7­3: Trade Goods). Obviously, merchants can sell these goods for
                 or services worth hundreds or thousands of gold pieces, the trans-            slightly more than they pay for them, but the difference is small
                 actions don't usually involve the exchange of that many individual            enough that you don't have to worry about it.
                 coins. Rather, the gold piece is a standard measure of value, and the
                 actual exchange is in gold bars, letters of credit, or valuable goods.                              WEAPONS

112           WEALTH OTHER THAN COINS

                 Most wealth is not in coins. It is livestock, grain, land, rights to col-
                 lect taxes, or rights to resources (such as a mine or a forest). Gems
                 and jewelry can also serve as portable wealth.

   Light: A light weapon is easier to use in one's off hand than a one-
handed weapon is, and it can be used while grappling. A light
weapon is used in one hand. Add the wielder's Strength bonus (if
any) to damage rolls for melee attacks with a light weapon if it's
used in the primary hand, or one-half the wielder's Strength bonus

                 creatures. The time it takes to get into or out of armor depends on     armor is automatically fatigued the next day. He or she takes a ­2
                 its type (see Table 7­7: Donning Armor).                                penalty on Strength and Dexterity and can't charge or run. Sleep-

              ARMOR QUALITIES
                                                                                            Arcane Spell Failure: Armor interferes with the gestures that
                 Armor isn't the only fashion statement a character can make, but        a spellcaster must make to cast an arcane spell that has a somatic
                 it's a big one. In addition, depending on class, a character may be     component. Arcane spellcasters face the possibility of arcane spell
                 proficient with all, some, or no armors, including shields. To wear     failure if they're wearing armor, so wizards and sorcerers usually

                 ciency feats (page 89), but most classes are automatically profi-       arcane spell failure chance for their bard spells.
                 cient with the armors that work best for them.

                    Armor and shields can take damage from some types of attacks         spell while wearing armor must usually make an arcane spell fail-

                    When selecting your character's armor, keep in mind the fol-         spell lacks a somatic component, however, it can be cast with no
                 lowing factors (given as column headings on Table 7­6: Armor            chance of arcane spell failure.
                 and Shields).

                    Cost: The cost of the armor for Small or Medium humanoid             two numbers together to get a single arcane spell failure chance.
                 creatures. See the Armor for Unusual Creatures sidebar for armor
                 prices for other creatures.                                                Speed: Medium or heavy armor slows the wearer down. It's
                                                                                         better to be slow and alive than to be quick and dead, but don't
                    Armor/Shield Bonus: Each armor grants an armor bonus to              neglect to give speed some thought. The number on Table 7­6 is

                 sents the protective value of the armor or shield. The armor bonus      half-elves, and half-orcs have an unencumbered speed of 30 feet.
                 from a suit of armor doesn't stack with other effects or items that     They use the first column. Dwarves, gnomes, and halflings have

   but they give you a bonus when attempting something you can                         When your current hit points drop to exactly 0, you're disabled.

   Most skill uses are standard actions, but some might be move                        damage after the completing the act. Unless your activity increased
   actions, full-round actions, free actions, or something else entirely.              your hit points, you are now at ­1 hit points, and you're dying.

   Your hit points measure how hard you are to kill. No matter how
   many hit points you lose, your character isn't hindered in any way                     You can also become disabled when recovering from dying. In
   until your hit points drop to 0 or lower.                                           this case, it's a step toward recovery, and you can have fewer than 0

LOSS OF HIT POINTS
                                                                                    DYING (­1 TO ­9 HIT POINTS)
   The most common way that your character gets hurt is to take
   lethal damage and lose hit points, whether from an orc's falchion, a                When your character's current hit points drop to between ­1 and
   wizard's lightning bolt spell, or a fall into molten lava. You record               ­9 inclusive, he's dying.
   your character's hit point total on your character sheet. As your
   character takes damage, you subtract that damage from your hit                         A dying character immediately falls unconscious and can take
   points, leaving you with your current hit points. Current hit points                no actions.
   go down when you take damage and go back up when you recover.
                                                                                          A dying character loses 1 hit point every round. This continues
      What Hit Points Represent: Hit points mean two things in                         until the character dies or becomes stable (see below).
   the game world: the ability to take physical punishment and keep
   going, and the ability to turn a serious blow into a less serious one.           DEAD (­10 HIT POINTS OR LOWER)
   For some characters, hit points may represent divine favor or
   inner power. When a paladin survives a fireball, you will be hard                   When your character's current hit points drop to ­10 or lower, or
   pressed to convince bystanders that she doesn't have the favor of                   if he takes massive damage (see above), he's dead. A character can
   some higher power.                                                                  also die from taking ability damage or suffering an ability drain
                                                                                       that reduces his Constitution to 0. When a character dies, his soul
                                                                                       immediately departs. Getting it back into the body is a major
                                                                                       hassle (see Bringing Back the Dead, page 171).

                                                                                    STABLE CHARACTERS AND RECOVERY

                                                                                       On the next turn after a character is reduced to between ­1 and ­9
                                                                                       hit points and on all subsequent turns, roll d% to see whether the
                                                                                       dying character becomes stable. He has a 10% chance of becoming
                                                                                       stable. If he doesn't, he loses 1 hit point. (A character who's uncon-
                                                                                       scious or dying can't use any special action that changes the initia-
                                                                                       tive count on which his action occurs.)

                                                                                          If the character's hit points drop to ­10 or lower, he's dead.
                                                                                          You can keep a dying character from losing any more hit points
                                                                                       and make him stable with a DC 15 Heal check.
                                                                                          If any sort of healing cures the dying character of even 1 point
                                                                                       of damage, he stops losing hit points and becomes stable.
                                                                                          Healing that raises the dying character's hit points to 0 makes

      Diagonals: When measuring distance, the first diagonal                                               to move around a corner,

   counts as 1 square, the second counts as 2 squares, the third counts                 while a Large creature has to use 3 squares
   as 1, the fourth as 2, and so on. (If it helps, you can think of a diag-
   onal as a distance of 1.5 squares.)                                                  (15 feet) of movement to move around a corner.

   est square or creature to a location, if two squares or creatures are
   equally close, randomly determine which one counts as closest by
   rolling a die.

                                                                                    Special Movement Rules
                     Difficult Terrain                                                 These rules cover special movement situations.
                                                                                          Accidentally Ending Movement in an Illegal Space: Some-
148                                                                                    times a character ends its movement while moving through a
                                                                                       space where it's not allowed to stop. For example, you might incur
                                                                                       an attack of opportunity from a monk while moving through a
                                                                                       friend's square and become stunned. When that happens, put your
                                                                                       miniature in the last legal position you occupied, or the closest
                                                                                       legal position, if there's a legal position that's closer.

                                                       COMBAT MODIFIERS

                                                Sometimes you just have to go toe-to-toe in a fight, but you can
                                                usually gain some advantage by seeking a better position, either
                                                offensively or defensively. This section covers the rules for when
                                                you can line up a particularly good attack or are forced to make a
                                                disadvantageous one.

                                             FAVORABLE AND
                                             UNFAVORABLE CONDITIONS

                                                Depending on the situation, you may gain bonuses or take penal-
                                                ties on your attack roll. Generally, any situational modifier created
                                                by the attacker's position or tactics applies to the attack roll, while
                                                any situational modifier created by the defender's position, state,
                                                or tactics applies to the defender's AC. Your DM judges what
                                                bonuses and penalties apply, using Table 8­5: Attack Roll Modi-

                                                     fiers and Table 8­6: Armor Class Modifiers as guides.

                                                            COVER

                                                                   One of the best defenses available is cover. By
                                                                    taking cover behind a tree, a wall, the side of a
                                                                    wagon, or the battlements of a castle, you can
                                                                    protect yourself from attacks, especially ranged

                                                                      attacks, and also from being spotted.
                                                                                 To determine whether your target has

                                                                            cover from your ranged attack, choose a
                                                                             corner of your square. If any line from
                                                                             this corner to any corner of the target's
                                                                             square passes through a square or border
                                                                              that blocks line of effect or provides
                                                                             cover, or through a square occupied by a

                                                                               creature, the target has cover (+4 to
                                                                                 AC).
                                                                                                          Creature Size
                                                                                                          Categories

150                    Colossal  Gargantuan  Huge  Large Medium Small Tiny

FLANKING
                                                                         troll
   When making a melee attack, you get a +2 flanking bonus if your
   opponent is threatened by a character or creature friendly to you                                Jozan
   on the opponent's opposite border or opposite corner.
                                                                                 Regdar Lidda
     When in doubt about whether two friendly characters flank an
   opponent in the middle, trace an imaginary line between the two               +2  +2
   friendly characters' centers. If the line passes through opposite
   borders of the opponent's space (including corners of those bor-                                                                             153
   ders), then the opponent is flanked.

      Exception: If a flanker takes up more than 1 square, it gets the
   flanking bonus if any square it occupies counts for flanking.

      Only a creature or character that threatens the defender can
   help an attacker get a flanking bonus.

      Creatures with a reach of 0 feet can't flank an opponent.

HELPLESS DEFENDERS

   A helpless opponent is someone who is bound, sleeping, para-
   lyzed, unconscious, or otherwise at your mercy.

      Regular Attack: A helpless character takes a ­4 penalty to AC
   against melee attacks, but no penalty to AC against ranged attacks.

            or multiply it by 2 for each size category larger than Medium.                  Avoiding Attacks of Opportunity: If you have the Improved Trip

            2 Varies by material; see Table 9­9, page 166.

158            Step 1: Attack of Opportunity. You provoke an attack of
            opportunity from the target whose weapon or shield you are
            trying to sunder. (If you have the Improved Sunder feat, you don't
            incur an attack of opportunity for making the attempt.)

               Step 2: Opposed Rolls. You and the defender make opposed
            attack rolls with your respective weapons. The wielder of a two-
            handed weapon on a sunder attempt gets a +4 bonus on this roll,
            and the wielder of a light weapon takes a ­4 penalty. If the com-
            batants are of different sizes, the larger combatant gets a bonus on
            the attack roll of +4 per difference in size category.

               Step 3: Consequences. If you beat the defender, you have
            landed a good blow. Roll damage and deal it to the weapon or
            shield. See Table 8­8: Common Armor, Weapon, and Shield Hard-
            ness and Hit Points to determine how much damage you must
            deal to destroy the weapon or shield.

               If you fail the sunder attempt, you don't deal any damage.
               Sundering a Carried or Worn Object: You don't use an opposed attack
            roll to damage a carried or worn object. Instead, just make an attack
            roll against the object's AC. A carried or worn object's AC is equal to
            10 + its size modifier + the Dexterity modifier of the carrying or
            wearing character. Attacking a carried or worn object provokes an
            attack of opportunity just as attacking a held object does. To attempt
            to snatch away an item worn by a defender (such as a cloak or a pair
   feat, or if you are tripping with a weapon (see below), you don't          Table 8­9: Turning Undead
   provoke an attack of opportunity for making a trip attack.

· Local, for exploring an area, measured in feet per minute.              Run: A character with a Constitution score of 9 or higher can                                  ADVENTURING

      Modes of Movement: While moving at the different move-                                                         Adventurers prepare for their
   ment scales, creatures generally walk, hustle, or run.                                                                    next challenge.

      Walk: A walk represents unhurried but purposeful move-                                                                                        Illus. by J. Jarvis
   ment at 3 miles per hour for an unencumbered human.

      Hustle: A hustle is a jog at about 6 miles per hour for an
   unencumbered human. A character moving his or her
   speed twice in a single round, or moving that speed in
   the same round that he or she performs a standard
   action or another move action is hustling when he or
   she moves.

      Run (×3): Moving three times speed is a running
   pace for a character in heavy armor. It represents about
   9 miles per hour for a human in full plate.

      Run (×4): Moving four times speed is a running pace
   for a character in light, medium, or no armor. It repre-
   sents about 12 miles per hour for an unencumbered
   human, or 8 miles per hour for a human in chainmail.

TACTICAL MOVEMENT

   Use tactical movement for combat, as detailed on
   page 147. Characters generally don't walk during
   combat--they hustle or run. A character who
   moves his or her speed and takes some action,
   such as attacking or casting a spell, is hustling for
   about half the round and doing something
   else the other half.

      Hampered Movement: Difficult ter-
   rain, obstacles, or poor visibility can
   hamper movement. When movement is
   hampered, each square moved into usu-
   ally counts as two squares, effectively
   reducing the distance that a character
   can cover in a move. For example, a
   character moving through difficult
   terrain (such as undergrowth) pays 2
   squares of movement per square
   moved into (double the normal cost).

      If more than one condition applies,
   multiply together all additional costs
   that apply. (This is a specific exception
   to the normal rule for doubling; see
   page 304.) For instance, a character
   moving through difficult terrain
   in darkness would pay 4 squares of
   movement per square moved into
   (double cost times double cost is
   quadruple cost).

      In some situations, your
   movement may be so hampered that
   you don't have sufficient speed even to move 5 feet
